walkthrough.title: Build the Island Frame
walkthrough.text: Let's get Catan set up. First, find the six blue sea frame pieces. Each piece has a number on the back. Match the numbers together so piece one connects to piece one, piece two to piece two, and so on. Snap them together on the table to form a large hexagonal border. This is the ocean surrounding your island.

walkthrough.title: Place the Terrain Hexes
walkthrough.text: Now grab the nineteen terrain hexes. These are the colorful tiles that make up the island. For your first game, use the beginner layout shown in the rulebook. Place each hex inside the frame according to the diagram. You will see four forests, four pastures, four fields, three hills, three mountains, and one desert.

walkthrough.title: Add the Number Tokens
walkthrough.text: Find the eighteen round number tokens. For the beginner layout, place them on the terrain hexes as shown in the rulebook diagram. Each token has a number and small dots underneath. More dots means that number gets rolled more often. The desert hex does not get a number token.

walkthrough.title: Place the Robber
walkthrough.text: Find the dark game piece that looks like a tall pawn. This is the robber. Place it on the desert hex to start the game. The robber will move around the board later when someone rolls a seven. For now, it just sits on the desert doing nothing.

walkthrough.title: Sort the Resource Cards
walkthrough.text: Separate the ninety-five resource cards into five face-up stacks by type. You will have brick, grain, lumber, ore, and wool. Place these stacks near the board where everyone can reach them. This is the bank. Players will draw from these stacks throughout the game.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Development Deck
walkthrough.text: Find the twenty-five development cards. These have a shield pattern on the back. Shuffle them face down and place the deck next to the resource stacks. Also place the two special cards, Longest Road and Largest Army, face up beside the board where everyone can see them.

walkthrough.title: Choose Colors and Take Pieces
walkthrough.text: Each player picks a color and takes all the pieces in that color. That means five settlements, four cities, and fifteen roads. Each player also takes one Building Costs reference card that matches their color. Keep your pieces in front of you where you can reach them easily.

walkthrough.title: Place Starting Settlements and Roads
walkthrough.text: For the beginner game, place your two starting settlements and two starting roads on the positions shown in the rulebook. Each settlement goes on an intersection where hex corners meet, and each road goes on an edge between two hexes. For your second settlement marked with a star, take one resource card from the bank for each terrain hex touching that settlement. Keep your resource cards hidden from other players.

walkthrough.title: Determine First Player
walkthrough.text: The oldest player goes first. Play continues clockwise from there. Grab the two dice and you are ready to begin. Each turn you will roll, collect resources, optionally trade, and optionally build. Let's learn the rules.

bullets: Connect 6 sea frame pieces by matching numbers (1-1, 2-2, etc.)

summary.title: Determine First Player
bullets: Oldest player goes first
Play proceeds clockwise
Grab 2 dice — ready to play

walkthrough.title: Goal of the Game
walkthrough.text: The goal of Catan is to be the first player to reach ten victory points on your turn. You earn points by building settlements worth one point each, upgrading them to cities worth two points each, and earning special cards like Longest Road and Largest Army. The game ends immediately when someone hits ten points during their own turn.

walkthrough.title: Turn Overview
walkthrough.text: Every turn follows the same four steps. First, you roll both dice. Second, everyone collects resources based on the number rolled. Third, you may trade resources with other players or with the bank. Fourth, you may build roads, settlements, cities, or buy development cards. Then you pass the dice clockwise.

walkthrough.title: Rolling and Producing Resources
walkthrough.text: Roll both dice and add them together. Every terrain hex with a matching number token produces resources for players who have a settlement or city touching that hex. A settlement earns one resource card of that terrain's type. A city earns two resource cards. Everyone collects at the same time, not just the player who rolled.

walkthrough.title: The Five Resource Types
walkthrough.text: There are five resources in Catan, and each one comes from a specific terrain. Forests produce lumber. Pastures produce wool. Fields produce grain. Hills produce brick. Mountains produce ore. The desert produces nothing. You will need different combinations of these resources to build things.

walkthrough.title: Rolling a Seven and the Robber
walkthrough.text: When a seven is rolled, no one collects resources. Instead, three things happen in order. First, any player holding more than seven resource cards must discard half of them, rounded down. Second, the player who rolled moves the robber to any other terrain hex. Third, that player steals one random resource card from any opponent who has a settlement or city touching the robber's new hex.

walkthrough.title: How the Robber Blocks Production
walkthrough.text: As long as the robber sits on a terrain hex, that hex does not produce resources for anyone. Even if the matching number is rolled, nothing happens on that hex. The robber stays put until someone rolls another seven or plays a Knight development card to move it somewhere else.

walkthrough.title: Trading Resources
walkthrough.text: After rolling, you may trade with other players. You can offer any combination of resources and negotiate freely. Only the active player can initiate and complete trades. Other players cannot trade among themselves. If no one wants to trade with you, you can always trade with the bank at a rate of four identical resources for any one resource you want.

walkthrough.title: Harbors and Better Trade Rates
walkthrough.text: If you build a settlement or city on a harbor intersection, you get a better trade rate with the bank. A generic harbor lets you trade three of any one resource for one of anything. A specific harbor, like a two-for-one brick harbor, lets you trade two of that specific resource for one of anything. Harbors can be incredibly powerful if your production is heavy in one resource.

walkthrough.title: Building Roads
walkthrough.text: A road costs one brick and one lumber. Place it on any open edge on the board that connects to one of your existing roads, settlements, or cities. Roads let you expand across the island toward new settlement spots. You have a maximum of fifteen roads.

walkthrough.title: Building Settlements
walkthrough.text: A settlement costs one brick, one lumber, one wool, and one grain. You must place it on an intersection that connects to one of your roads. Here is the critical rule: the Distance Rule says your settlement must be at least two intersections away from any other settlement or city, including your own. This means every adjacent intersection must be empty.

walkthrough.title: Upgrading to Cities
walkthrough.text: A city costs three ore and two grain. To build one, you replace one of your existing settlements with a city piece. Cities are worth two victory points instead of one, and they produce two resource cards instead of one whenever their hex number is rolled. You can have a maximum of four cities.

walkthrough.title: Buying Development Cards
walkthrough.text: A development card costs one ore, one wool, and one grain. Draw the top card from the development deck and keep it hidden. There are three types: Knight cards let you move the robber. Progress cards give you special one-time effects like free roads or free resources. Victory Point cards are worth one point each and stay hidden until you reveal them to win.

walkthrough.title: Longest Road and Largest Army
walkthrough.text: Two special bonus cards are worth two victory points each. Longest Road goes to the first player who builds a continuous road of at least five segments. Largest Army goes to the first player who plays at least three Knight cards. These cards can change hands if another player surpasses you. Losing one of these cards is a four-point swing, so keep an eye on the competition.

walkthrough.title: Winning the Game
walkthrough.text: You win by reaching ten or more victory points during your own turn. Add up your settlements, cities, any special cards you hold, and any revealed Victory Point development cards. The moment you hit ten on your turn, announce it and the game is over. If you reach ten points during someone else's turn, you must wait until your own turn to claim victory.

summary.title: Goal of the Game
bullets: First to 10 VP on your turn wins immediately
VP sources: settlements (1), cities (2), Longest Road (2), Largest Army (2), VP dev cards (1 each)

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Catan
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first game of Catan. In this practice tutorial, we will walk through several turns with three example players to show you how the game flows. We will cover rolling for resources, trading, building, and common mistakes new players make. Follow along even if your player count is different.

walkthrough.title: The Board Is Set
walkthrough.text: We are using the beginner layout. Each player has placed their two starting settlements and two roads. Player one has settlements touching a six and an eight, which are the most commonly rolled numbers. Player two is on a five, a nine, and a ten. Player three has a mix of mid-range numbers. Everyone has collected starting resources from their starred settlement.

walkthrough.title: Player One Rolls and Collects
walkthrough.text: Player one rolls the dice and gets an eight. Everyone checks the board for hexes with the number eight token. Player one has a settlement touching an eight, so they take one resource card of that type from the bank. Player three also has a settlement on an eight, so they collect too. Remember, all players collect on every roll, not just the person who rolled.

walkthrough.title: Player One Builds a Road
walkthrough.text: Player one has a brick and a lumber in hand. That is exactly the cost of one road. They place a road extending from one of their settlements toward an empty intersection where they plan to build a future settlement. Always build roads with a destination in mind.

walkthrough.title: Player Two Rolls and Trades
walkthrough.text: Player two rolls a five and collects wool from a pasture hex. They now have three wool but no brick or lumber. Player two asks the table if anyone wants to trade wool for brick. Player three agrees to swap one wool for one brick. Only the active player can make trades, so deals happen on Player two's turn.

walkthrough.title: Mistake: Hoarding Too Many Cards
walkthrough.text: Player three has been saving up resources for several turns. They now hold nine cards, hoping to buy a city next turn. Player one rolls a seven. Since Player three has more than seven cards, they must immediately discard half, rounded down. That means discarding four cards. Player three just lost almost half their hand to the robber.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Spend Before You Lose It
walkthrough.text: The takeaway is simple. Do not hoard resources. If you are holding eight or more cards, a seven can wipe out half your hand. Spend your resources as soon as you can put them to good use. Build a road, buy a development card, or make a trade. Anything is better than losing cards to the robber discard.

walkthrough.title: Resolving the Robber
walkthrough.text: After discards are handled, Player one moves the robber off the desert and onto a mountain hex that Player two relies on for ore. Now that hex will not produce ore for anyone until the robber is moved again. Player one also steals one random card from Player two's hand. The robber is a powerful weapon.

walkthrough.title: Mistake: Building Roads to Nowhere
walkthrough.text: Player two wants to expand quickly, so they build two roads in a row heading toward a nice-looking intersection. But when they check the Distance Rule, they realize the intersection they were aiming for is only one space away from Player three's settlement. They cannot build a settlement there. Those two roads are wasted.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Check the Distance Rule First
walkthrough.text: Before you build any road, look at your destination intersection and make sure all three adjacent intersections are empty. If any neighboring spot has a settlement or city belonging to any player, you cannot build there. Plan your road path before you spend your resources. One wasted road can set you back several turns.

walkthrough.title: Mistake: Trading with the Leader
walkthrough.text: Player one is at seven points and needs ore to build a city for the win. They offer Player three a generous trade: two lumber for one ore. Player three thinks it sounds like a great deal and accepts. Player one immediately upgrades a settlement to a city and reaches nine points. One more point and they win.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Watch the Scoreboard
walkthrough.text: Always check how many points each player has before you agree to a trade. A trade that seems fair on resources can be terrible strategically if it helps the leader get closer to winning. When someone is at seven or eight points, think twice before giving them anything. It is often better to take a worse deal from a player who is behind.

walkthrough.title: Mistake: Ignoring Development Cards
walkthrough.text: Player three has been focusing entirely on building settlements and roads. They have not bought a single development card all game. Meanwhile, Player two has quietly played three Knights to claim the Largest Army card, earning two free victory points. Player three had the ore, wool, and grain to buy development cards but never considered it.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Development Cards Win Games
walkthrough.text: Development cards are one of the most efficient paths to victory. Knights let you control the robber and work toward the two-point Largest Army bonus. Victory Point cards are hidden points your opponents cannot see coming. Progress cards can give you free roads or free resources. Do not ignore the development deck.

walkthrough.title: Player Two Plays Smart
walkthrough.text: Player two has been building steadily. They have three settlements, the Largest Army card from their Knights, and a hidden Victory Point development card. That is three plus two plus one, giving them six visible points and one secret point. They build a road toward a harbor to improve their trading efficiency for the late game.

walkthrough.title: The Power of Harbors
walkthrough.text: Player two reaches a two-for-one grain harbor. They produce a lot of grain from their fields settlements but struggle to get ore. Now they can trade just two grain for any one resource they need through the harbor. This is far better than the default four-for-one bank rate. Harbors are especially powerful when your production is lopsided toward one resource.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Congratulations, you have walked through the core flow of Catan. You have seen how rolling produces resources for everyone, how to trade and build, and how the robber punishes card hoarders. You also learned four common mistakes: hoarding too many cards, building roads without checking the Distance Rule, trading with the leader, and ignoring development cards. You are ready to play a full game.

walkthrough.title: Before You Settle
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy basics and resource priorities.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to building costs, development cards, and key terms.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

Reference the Appendix → building costs, dev cards, key terms
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we talk strategy, here are the most common mistakes new Catan players make. First, hoarding eight or more resource cards and losing half to a seven. Second, building roads toward intersections that violate the Distance Rule. Third, making trades that help the leading player win. Fourth, ignoring development cards entirely and missing out on Largest Army and hidden victory points. Keep these in mind and you will avoid the biggest beginner traps.

walkthrough.title: Starting Settlement Placement
walkthrough.text: Your starting settlement placement is the most important decision in the entire game. Prioritize intersections that touch hexes with six or eight tokens, because those numbers are rolled most often. Try to cover at least four different resource types across your two settlements. If you cannot get all five resources, make sure you have access to brick and lumber for early roads and settlements. A settlement on a great number with a resource you cannot use is worse than a decent number with a resource you desperately need.

walkthrough.title: Resource Priority in the Early Game
walkthrough.text: In the early game, brick and lumber are king. You need them for roads and settlements, which are how you expand across the board and claim new territory. Ore and grain become critical later when you start building cities and buying development cards. Wool is the most flexible resource because it appears in both settlement and development card costs. Make sure your starting settlements give you early access to brick and lumber, or you will watch other players expand while you sit stuck.

walkthrough.title: Manage Your Hand Size
walkthrough.text: The seven-card robber rule is one of the most punishing mechanics in Catan. Every time a seven is rolled, anyone with eight or more cards loses half. On average, a seven comes up once every six rolls. That means if you are sitting on a fat hand of resources, you are gambling. Spend your cards as soon as you have a productive use for them. If you cannot build anything useful, trade down or buy a development card to keep your hand lean.

walkthrough.title: Trading Smart
walkthrough.text: Trading is one of the most powerful tools in Catan, but it requires discipline. Never trade with the player who is closest to winning unless you absolutely have to. Always count their visible victory points before agreeing to any deal. When you are offering trades, try to get what you need without revealing your exact plans. And remember, you can always trade with the bank at four-for-one, or better rates if you control a harbor.

walkthrough.title: The Value of Development Cards
walkthrough.text: New players often overlook the development deck, but it contains some of the most efficient victory points in the game. Three Knights earn you the Largest Army card for two free points. Each hidden Victory Point card is one point your opponents cannot see or counter. Progress cards like Road Building give you two free roads, saving you resources. Buying one or two development cards per game is almost always worth it.

walkthrough.title: Expand or Upgrade
walkthrough.text: At some point in the game, you face a critical choice: do you keep building new settlements, or start upgrading to cities? Settlements let you touch new hexes and diversify your resource income. Cities double the production of hexes you already control. The answer depends on your board position. If your settlements are on high-value numbers like six and eight, upgrade early to double that production. If your numbers are weaker, expand to new hexes with better numbers instead.

walkthrough.title: Watch the Endgame
walkthrough.text: Catan games are often decided in the last two or three turns. Pay attention to every player's visible point count. Remember that hidden Victory Point development cards can surprise you. If someone is at eight visible points, they could be at nine or ten with hidden cards. When you are close to winning, avoid telegraphing your plans. Build your winning move in one turn if possible so opponents cannot block you with the robber or refuse trades.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't hold 8+ cards — a 7 discards half your hand
Don't build roads without checking Distance Rule at destination
Don't trade with the leader — check VP count first
Don't ignore development cards — Knights and VP cards are efficient

Good number + needed resource > great number + useless resource

6/8 hexes → upgrade early to double output

summary.title: Watch the Endgame

At 8 visible VP, opponent may already be at 9-10
Build your winning move in one turn to prevent blocks

walkthrough.title: Number Probability and Hex Valuation
walkthrough.text: Understanding dice probability is the foundation of advanced Catan play. With two dice, a six or eight is rolled five times out of thirty-six rolls. A five or nine comes up four times. A four or ten hits three times. A three or eleven appears twice, and a two or twelve shows up only once. The dots printed under each number token tell you this at a glance. When placing settlements, add up the dots on all adjacent hexes to compare total production value.

walkthrough.title: The Ore-Grain Engine
walkthrough.text: One of the strongest strategies in Catan is the ore-grain engine. If your starting settlements give you strong ore and grain production, you can rapidly build cities and buy development cards. Cities on your ore and grain hexes double their output, creating a compounding effect. Three ore and two grain builds a city. One ore, one wool, and one grain buys a development card. This path often leads to Largest Army plus two or three hidden Victory Point cards.

walkthrough.title: The Brick-Lumber Rush
walkthrough.text: The opposite approach is the brick-lumber rush. If your early production is heavy in brick and lumber, lean into road and settlement expansion. Build roads aggressively toward the best unclaimed intersections. Every new settlement increases your resource diversity and gives you another production hex. This strategy also naturally pursues Longest Road. Five continuous road segments earn you two bonus points, and extending beyond five makes it harder for anyone to steal the card from you.

walkthrough.title: Harbor Strategy
walkthrough.text: Harbors are underrated by most new players but are a cornerstone of advanced play. If your production is heavily skewed toward one resource, building a settlement on the matching two-for-one harbor turns that surplus into anything you need. For example, if you have two settlements on strong grain hexes, a two-for-one grain harbor lets you convert every two grain into any resource. This effectively makes you self-sufficient and less dependent on player trades, which becomes critical in the late game when opponents refuse to deal with you.

walkthrough.title: Robber Strategy and Knight Timing
walkthrough.text: The robber is not just a nuisance. It is a strategic weapon. When you control the robber through Knight cards, place it on the highest-value hex of the leading player. Blocking their six or eight hex can slow their engine significantly. Save your Knight cards for moments when the robber is on your own hexes or when you need to deny a critical resource to a competitor. Playing three Knights also earns Largest Army. If you are pursuing this, try to play your third Knight on a turn where you can also build, making it harder for opponents to react.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Table and Threat Assessment
walkthrough.text: Advanced Catan is as much about reading opponents as it is about building your own engine. Track every player's visible points, their likely hidden Victory Point cards, and their resource production. If someone has eight visible points and has bought three development cards, assume they have at least one hidden victory point card. Adjust your robber placement and trade decisions accordingly. The player in last place is your best trading partner. The player in first place should get nothing from you.

walkthrough.title: Blocking and Denial
walkthrough.text: Sometimes the best move in Catan is not building for yourself but preventing your opponent from building. If you see an opponent aiming for a specific intersection, racing to build a settlement there first can deny them a critical expansion point. The Distance Rule means your settlement blocks their settlement from all adjacent intersections too. Similarly, building a road that cuts off an opponent's road path can strand their expansion plan. Just make sure your blocking move still advances your own position.

walkthrough.title: Closing Out the Win
walkthrough.text: The hardest part of Catan is converting eight or nine points into ten. At this stage, every opponent will refuse to trade with you and will target you with the robber. Plan your endgame two or three turns in advance. Accumulate the exact resources you need in one turn cycle. If you have hidden Victory Point cards, do not reveal them until you can also build or play a card to reach ten on the same turn. The element of surprise wins more games of Catan than any other single factor.

summary.title: Number Probability and Hex Valuation
bullets: 6/8 = 5/36 chance each (most common after 7)
Dots under number token = relative frequency
Sum dots on adjacent hexes to compare intersection value

summary.title: The Ore-Grain Engine
bullets: Strong Ore + Grain → cities + dev cards
Cities on Ore/Grain hexes → double output, compounding
City = 3 Ore + 2 Grain; Dev card = 1 Ore + 1 Wool + 1 Grain
Path to Largest Army + hidden VP cards

summary.title: The Brick-Lumber Rush
bullets: Heavy Brick/Lumber → fast road + settlement expansion
New settlements = more hex coverage and resource diversity
Naturally pursues Longest Road (5+ segments = 2 VP)
Extend past 5 to make it harder to steal

summary.title: Harbor Strategy
bullets: 2:1 harbor for your surplus resource = self-sufficiency
Less dependent on player trades in late game
Worth spending early roads to reach a matching harbor
Generic 3:1 harbor also strong if production is mixed

summary.title: Robber Strategy and Knight Timing
bullets: Place robber on leader's 6/8 hex for maximum impact
Save Knights for when robber is on your hex or to deny leader
3 Knights → Largest Army (2 VP)
Play 3rd Knight + build on same turn to prevent reaction

summary.title: Reading the Table and Threat Assessment
bullets: Track visible VP + likely hidden VP cards for each player
8 VP + 3 dev card purchases → assume 9-10 real VP
Trade with last-place player, starve first-place player
Adjust robber and trades based on threat level

summary.title: Blocking and Denial
bullets: Race to claim intersections opponents are targeting
Your settlement blocks all 3 adjacent intersections via Distance Rule
Cut off road paths to strand opponent's expansion
Only block if it also advances your own position

summary.title: Closing Out the Win
bullets: At 8-9 VP: opponents refuse trades and target you with robber
Plan endgame 2-3 turns ahead
Accumulate exact resources in one turn cycle
Hidden VP cards + build on same turn = surprise win

entries.definition: A player building placed on an intersection. Costs 1 Brick, 1 Lumber, 1 Wool, and 1 Grain to build. Worth 1 victory point and produces 1 resource card when an adjacent hex's number is rolled. Each player has a maximum of 5 settlements.

An upgraded settlement placed by replacing an existing settlement. Costs 3 Ore and 2 Grain. Worth 2 victory points and produces 2 resource cards when an adjacent hex's number is rolled. Each player has a maximum of 4 cities.

A player piece placed on an edge between two intersections. Costs 1 Brick and 1 Lumber. Must connect to an existing road, settlement, or city belonging to you. Each player has a maximum of 15 roads. Roads enable expansion toward new settlement locations.
A resource produced by Hill hexes. Used to build roads (with Lumber) and settlements. One of the two critical early-game resources for expansion.
A resource produced by Forest hexes. Used to build roads (with Brick) and settlements. Paired with Brick as the primary expansion resource.
A resource produced by Pasture hexes. Used to build settlements and buy development cards. The most versatile resource, appearing in two different build costs.
A resource produced by Fields hexes. Used to build settlements, upgrade to cities, and buy development cards. Critical in the mid-to-late game for cities and development cards.
A resource produced by Mountain hexes. Used to upgrade settlements to cities and buy development cards. Ore becomes the most valuable resource once expansion slows and the city-building phase begins.

A placement restriction requiring that every new settlement be at least two intersections away from any existing settlement or city on the board, regardless of which player owns it. All three adjacent intersections must be empty.

A game piece that blocks resource production on the hex it occupies. Activated when a 7 is rolled or a Knight card is played. The player who moves the robber steals one random resource card from an opponent with a settlement or city adjacent to the new hex.
When a 7 is rolled, every player holding more than 7 resource cards must discard half their hand, rounded down. This is resolved before the active player moves the robber.

A trade between the active player and any other player. Only the player whose turn it is may initiate and complete trades. Non-active players cannot trade with each other. Any combination of resources can be offered.
Trading 4 identical resource cards to the bank in exchange for 1 resource card of any type. This is always available regardless of other players' willingness to trade. Harbors can improve this rate.
A harbor that allows a player to trade any 3 identical resources to the bank for 1 resource of any type. Requires a settlement or city built on the harbor intersection.

A harbor that allows a player to trade 2 of the pictured resource type to the bank for 1 resource of any type. Requires a settlement or city built on the harbor intersection. Much more efficient than bank or generic harbor trades.

The most common development card (14 in the deck). When played, the player moves the robber to any hex and steals one random resource from an adjacent opponent. Played Knights are kept face-up. Three or more Knights can earn the Largest Army card.

A development card worth 1 hidden victory point (5 in the deck). Kept secret until the holder reveals it to prove they have reached 10 or more victory points on their turn. Cannot be stolen or traded.

A development card with a one-time special effect (6 in the deck). Types include Road Building (place 2 free roads), Year of Plenty (take any 2 resources from the bank), and Monopoly (name a resource and all opponents must give you all cards of that type). Discarded after use.

A special card worth 2 victory points awarded to the first player who builds a continuous road of at least 5 segments. The road must be unbroken and can include forks, but only the longest continuous path counts. If another player builds a strictly longer continuous road, the card transfers immediately.

A special card worth 2 victory points awarded to the first player who has played at least 3 Knight development cards. If another player plays more Knights, the card transfers immediately. Losing this card creates a 4-point swing.

A player may play at most 1 development card per turn, and it cannot be a card purchased on that same turn. Victory Point cards are the exception and are only revealed when claiming victory. Knights may be played before or after rolling.

If a dice roll would produce a resource but the bank does not have enough cards of that type to pay all eligible players, no one receives that resource for that roll. Other resources from the same roll are still distributed normally.

The small dots printed beneath each number on a number token indicate the relative probability of that number being rolled. More dots means more frequent production. Six and eight have 5 dots each, while two and twelve have only 1 dot each.

A reference card given to each player showing the resource costs for all buildable items: Road (1 Brick + 1 Lumber), Settlement (1 Brick + 1 Lumber + 1 Wool + 1 Grain), City (3 Ore + 2 Grain), and Development Card (1 Ore + 1 Wool + 1 Grain).
